title: Ensure that Dataproc Cluster is encrypted using Customer- Managed Encryption Key

input_row.title: Ensure that Dataproc Cluster is encrypted using Customer- Managed Encryption Key
input_row.description: When you use Dataproc, cluster and job data is stored on Persistent Disks (PDs) associated with the Compute Engine VMs in your cluster and in a Cloud Storage staging bucket. This PD and bucket data is encrypted using a Google-generated data encryption key (DEK) and key encryption key (KEK). The CMEK feature allows you to create, use, and revoke the key encryption key (KEK). Google still controls the data encryption key (DEK).
input_row.rationale: "Cloud services offer the ability to protect data related to those services using encryption keys managed by the customer within Cloud KMS. These encryption keys are called customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK). When you protect data in Google Cloud services with CMEK, the CMEK key is within your control.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Login to the GCP Console and navigate to the Dataproc Cluster page by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/dataproc/clusters. 2. Select the project from the project dropdown list. 3. On the Dataproc Clusters page, select the cluster and click on the Name attribute value that you want to examine. 4. On the details page, select the Configurations tab. 5. On the Configurations tab, check the Encryption type configuration attribute value. If the value is set to Google-managed key, then Dataproc Cluster is not encrypted with Customer managed encryption keys. Repeat step no. 3 - 5 for other Dataproc Clusters available in the selected project. 6. Change the project from the project dropdown list and repeat the audit procedure for other projects. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Run clusters list command to list all the Dataproc Clusters available in the region: gcloud dataproc clusters list --region='us-central1' 2. Run clusters describe command to get the key details of the selected cluster: gcloud dataproc clusters describe --region=us-central1 -- flatten=config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName 3. If the above command output return "null", then the selected cluster is not encrypted with Customer managed encryption keys. 4. Repeat step no. 2 and 3 for other Dataproc Clusters available in the selected region. Change the region by updating --region and repeat step no. 2 for other clusters available in the project. Change the project by running the below command and repeat the audit procedure for other Dataproc clusters available in other projects: gcloud config set project "
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Login to the GCP Console and navigate to the Dataproc Cluster page by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/dataproc/clusters. 2. Select the project from the projects dropdown list. 3. On the Dataproc Cluster page, click on the Create Cluster to create a new cluster with Customer managed encryption keys. 4. On Create a cluster page, perform below steps: • Inside Set up cluster section perform below steps: -In the Name textbox, provide a name for your cluster. o From Location select the location in which you want to deploy a cluster. o Configure other configurations as per your requirements. • Inside Configure Nodes and Customize cluster section configure the settings as per your requirements. • Inside Manage security section, perform below steps: o From Encryption, select Customer-managed key. o Select a customer-managed key from dropdown list. o Ensure that the selected KMS Key have Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role assign to Dataproc Cluster service account ("serviceAccount:service-@compute- system.iam.gserviceaccount.com"). o Click on Create to create a cluster. • Once the cluster is created migrate all your workloads from the older cluster to the new cluster and delete the old cluster by performing the below steps: o On the Clusters page, select the old cluster and click on Delete cluster. o On the Confirm deletion window, click on Confirm to delete the cluster. o Repeat step above for other Dataproc clusters available in the selected project. • Change the project from the project dropdown list and repeat the remediation procedure for other Dataproc clusters available in other projects. From Google Cloud CLI Before creating cluster ensure that the selected KMS Key have Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role assign to Dataproc Cluster service account ("serviceAccount:service-@compute- system.iam.gserviceaccount.com"). Run clusters create command to create new cluster with customer-managed key: gcloud dataproc clusters create --region=us-central1 --gce-pd- kms-key= The above command will create a new cluster in the selected region. Once the cluster is created migrate all your workloads from the older cluster to the new cluster and Run clusters delete command to delete cluster: gcloud dataproc clusters delete --region=us-central1 Repeat step no. 1 to create a new Dataproc cluster. Change the project by running the below command and repeat the remediation procedure for other projects: gcloud config set project "
input_row.impact: Using Customer Managed Keys involves additional overhead in maintenance by administrators.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit control includes detailed CLI commands that can be executed to check the encryption status of Dataproc Clusters, utilizing the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud).
- All required properties can be queried via GCP APIs or GCP CLI commands, confirming that the configuration is accessible programmatically.
- Since this is a standard service property regarding encryption for storage, it fits the criteria for automation.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_dataproc_cluster_encryption_customer_managed_keys_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the GCP CLI command `gcloud dataproc clusters list --region='us-central1'` to enumerate all Dataproc clusters in specified regions.
- For each cluster, use `gcloud dataproc clusters describe --region=us-central1 --project=[PROJECT_ID] [CLUSTER_NAME]` to retrieve the encryption configuration, specifically examining the `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` attribute.
- Pass/Fail criteria:
  - If the output of the above command indicates that `gcePdKmsKeyName` is set to `null`, the cluster is not using Customer Managed Encryption Keys.
  - If it contains a valid key name, then the cluster is correctly configured to use Customer Managed Encryption Keys.
- Multi-project audits can be facilitated by repeatedly altering the `--project` parameter to check clusters in different projects.

This approach ensures comprehensive coverage across the resources, validating compliance with the CIS control relating to Dataproc cluster encryption using Customer Managed Encryption Keys.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification of this control as automated is correct. The audit can be conducted using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and CLI commands, which allows for programmatic access to the required configuration details.
- **GCP APIs/Google Cloud SDK (gcloud)/CLI:** The assessment correctly identifies that the necessary information can be accessed via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The use of `gcloud dataproc clusters describe` is appropriate for retrieving encryption configuration details.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- **Automation Details:** The automation details provided are technically accurate. The use of `gcloud dataproc clusters list` to enumerate clusters and `gcloud dataproc clusters describe` to check the encryption configuration is correct. The check for the `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` attribute is a valid method to determine if Customer Managed Encryption Keys are being used.
- **Program Name:** The program name `gcp_dataproc_cluster_encryption_customer_managed_keys_enabled` is descriptive and follows the convention. However, it could be slightly improved for clarity by including "pd" to specify persistent disk encryption.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `gcp_dataproc_cluster_pd_encryption_cmek_enabled` to explicitly indicate that the check is for persistent disk encryption using Customer Managed Encryption Keys.
- **Missing Considerations:** Ensure that the script accounts for all regions and projects by iterating over them, as clusters can exist in multiple regions and projects. Additionally, consider handling API rate limits and errors gracefully.
- **Alternative Methods:** The current method is efficient, but for large-scale environments, consider using GCP's Asset Inventory API to list resources and their configurations, which might offer performance benefits.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of GCP CLI commands is appropriate for the task. The technical details are accurate and align with GCP's capabilities.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modification:** Update the program name to `gcp_dataproc_cluster_pd_encryption_cmek_enabled` for enhanced clarity.
- The rest of the assessment is solid and should remain as-is, with the suggested improvements implemented for better coverage and clarity.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**
Both AI opinions agree that the audit can be automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The necessary encryption configuration details for Dataproc Clusters are accessible programmatically via GCP APIs, specifically using the `gcloud dataproc clusters describe` command to check the `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName` attribute. This confirms that the control can be effectively automated.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
gcp_dataproc_cluster_pd_encryption_cmek_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: Dataproc Clusters
- API/CLI Method: Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) `dataproc clusters describe`
- Properties to Check: `config.encryptionConfig.gcePdKmsKeyName`
- Pass Criteria: The `gcePdKmsKeyName` attribute contains a valid key name, indicating the use of Customer Managed Encryption Keys.
- Fail Criteria: The `gcePdKmsKeyName` attribute is set to `null`, indicating that Customer Managed Encryption Keys are not being used.

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**
There were no disagreements between the AI opinions; both supported the automated approach. The second opinion suggested a slight improvement in the program name for clarity, which has been adopted.

**SOURCE:** CIS_Google_Cloud_Platform_Foundation_Benchmark_v4_0_0
